subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following compounds formed by Cu 2+ ions is most stable?
question_html: Which of the following compounds formed by Cu2+ ions is most stable?

options.explanation: Cu 2+ + 4CN - {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} [Cu(CN) 4 ] 2- ; Log K = 27.3 K is called the equilibrium constant( or binding constant) and tells us how strongly the ligands bind to the metal centre. So, the higher the value of K, the greater the stability of the complex formed. {tex}K= \frac {[Cu(CN)_4]}{[Cu]^{2+}{[CN]^-}^{4}}{/tex} Greater the value of K, the equilibrium is favoured more towards the side of the formation of complex, which is possible only if it is very stable.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: The colour of the coordination compounds depends on the crystals field splitting. What will be the correct order of absorption of warleinth of light in the visible region. for the complexes [CO(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ ; [CO(CN) 0 ] 3- ; [CO(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ .
question_html: The colour of the coordination compounds depends on the crystals field splitting. What will be the correct order of absorption of warleinth of light in the visible region. for the complexes [CO(NH3)6]3+; [CO(CN)0]3-; [CO(H2O)6]3+.

options.explanation: Stronger the ligand, stronger the crystal field stabilization energy, higher the wavelength of Absorption. CN - >NH 3 >H 2 O [Strength of Ligands] So, {tex}[CO(CN)_6]^{3-}>[CO(NH_3)_6]^{3+}>[CO(H_2O)_6]^{3+}{/tex} is the order of absorption of the wavelength of light.

question_text: The stabilisation of coordination compounds due to chelation is called the chelate effect. Which of the following is the most stable complex species?
question_html: The stabilisation of coordination compounds due to chelation is called the chelate effect. Which of the following is the most stable complex species?

options.explanation: Chelation (formation of the cycle by the linkage between the metal ion and ligand) stabilizes the coordination compound. The ligand which chelates the metal ion is known as a chelating ligand. Here, only [Fe(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3+ s a coordination compound which contains oxalate ion as a chelating ligand. Hence, it stabilizes the coordination compound by chelating Fe 3+ ion.

options.explanation: [Cr(H 2 O) 4 Cl 2 ] + shows geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism. In cis isomer, two ClCl ligands are adjacent to each other. In trans isomer, two ClCl ligands are opposite to each other. Complexes of type MA 4 ​B 2 ​ shows geometrical isomerism.

options.explanation: Crystal field splitting energy for tetrahedral is 4/9 times that for octahedral {tex}∆t= \frac{4}{9} ∆_o{/tex} {tex}∆t= \frac{4}{9} ×18,000 cm^{–1}=8,000 cm^{–1}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Atomic number of Mn, Fe and Co are 25, 26 and 27 respectively. Which of the following inner orbital octahedral complex ions are diamagnetic? [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [Mn(CN) 6 ] 3- [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4- [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3-
question_html:
Atomic number of Mn, Fe and Co are 25, 26 and 27 respectively. Which of the following inner orbital octahedral complex ions are diamagnetic?

  1. [Co(NH3)6]3+
  2. [Mn(CN)6]3-
  3. [Fe(CN)6]4-
  4. [Fe(CN)6]3-
solution_text: (i, iii) Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Co 3+ in [CoNH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ is Number of unpaired electron = 0 Magnetic property of compound = Diamagnetic Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Mn 3+ in [Mn(CN) 6 ] 3- Number of unpaired electron = 2 Magnetic property of compound = Paramagnetic Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Fe 2+ in [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4- is Number of unpaired electron = 0 Magnetic property of compound = Diamagnetic Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Fe 3+ in [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- Number of unpaired electron = 1 Magnetic property of compound = Paramagnetic
solution_html:
(i, iii)

  1. Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Co3+ in [CoNH3)6]3+ is

    Number of unpaired electron = 0
    Magnetic property of compound = Diamagnetic
  2. Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Mn3+ in [Mn(CN)6]3-

    Number of unpaired electron = 2
    Magnetic property of compound = Paramagnetic
  3. Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Fe2+ in [Fe(CN)6]4- is

    Number of unpaired electron = 0
    Magnetic property of compound = Diamagnetic
  4. Molecular orbital electronic configuration of Fe3+ in [Fe(CN)6]3-

    Number of unpaired electron = 1
    Magnetic property of compound = Paramagnetic

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: An aqueous pink solution of cobalt(II) chloride changes to deep blue on addition of excess of HCl. This is because ________. [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ is transformed into [CoCl 6 ] 4- [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ is transformed into [CoCl 4 ] 2- tetrahedral complexes have smaller crystal field splitting than octahedral complexes. tetrahedral complexes have larger crystal field splitting than octahedral complex.
question_html:
An aqueous pink solution of cobalt(II) chloride changes to deep blue on addition of excess of HCl. This is because ________.

  1. [Co(H2O)6]2+ is transformed into [CoCl6]4-
  2. [Co(H2O)6]2+ is transformed into [CoCl4]2-
  3. tetrahedral complexes have smaller crystal field splitting than octahedral complexes.
  4. tetrahedral complexes have larger crystal field splitting than octahedral complex.
solution_text: Option (ii, iii) The aqueous pink solution of cobalt (II) chloride is due to the electronic transition of an electron from t 2g to e g energy level of [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ complex. When an excess of HCl is added to this solution [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ is transformed into [CoCl 4 ] 2- Tetrahedral complexes have smaller crystal field splitting than octahedral complexes because {tex}\Delta_{r}=\frac{4}{9} \Delta_{0}{/tex}
solution_html:
Option (ii, iii) The aqueous pink solution of cobalt (II) chloride is due to the electronic transition of an electron from t2g to eg energy level of [Co(H2O)6]2+ complex. When an excess of HCl is added to this solution

  1. [Co(H2O)6]2+is transformed into [CoCl4]2-
  2. Tetrahedral complexes have smaller crystal field splitting than octahedral complexes because {tex}\Delta_{r}=\frac{4}{9} \Delta_{0}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following complexes are homoleptic? [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [Co(NH 3 )4Cl 2 ] + [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- [Ni(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]
question_html:
Which of the following complexes are homoleptic?

  1. [Co(NH3)6]3+
  2. [Co(NH3)4Cl2]+
  3. [Ni(CN)4]2-
  4. [Ni(NH3)4Cl2]
solution_text: (i, iii) In complexes [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ and [Ni(CN) 4 ] 2- , both metals Co and Ni are attached to one kind of ligands (donor group) only hence, they are homoleptic.
solution_html: (i, iii) In complexes [Co(NH3)6]3+ and [Ni(CN)4]2-, both metals Co and Ni are attached to one kind of ligands (donor group) only hence, they are homoleptic.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Which of the following complexes are heteroleptic? [Cr(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ [Fe(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ] + [Mn(CN) 6 ] 4- [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]
question_html:
Which of the following complexes are heteroleptic?

  1. [Cr(NH3)6]3+
  2. [Fe(NH3)4Cl2]+
  3. [Mn(CN)6]4-
  4. [Co(NH3)4Cl2]
solution_text: (ii, iv) In complexes, [Fe(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ] + and [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ], metal is bonded to more than one kind of ligands (donor group) hence, they are heteroleptic.
solution_html: (ii, iv) In complexes, [Fe(NH3)4Cl2]+ and [Co(NH3)4Cl2], metal is bonded to more than one kind of ligands (donor group) hence, they are heteroleptic.

question_html:

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: [CO(NH 3 ) 3 Cl 3 ], [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]Cl, [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 , [Cr(NH 3 ) 5 Cl]Cl 2
question_html: Arrange the following complexes in the increasing order of conductivity of their solution: [CO(NH3)3Cl3], [Co(NH3)4Cl2]Cl, [Co(NH3)6]Cl3, [Cr(NH3)5Cl]Cl2
solution_text: [CO(NH 3 ) 3 Cl 3 ] - does not ionize [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]Cl = [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ] + + Cl - (ionize to give 2 ions) [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3 = [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ + 3Cl - (ionize to give 4 ions) [Co(NH 3 ) 5 Cl]Cl 2 = [Co(NH 3 ) 5 Cl] 2 + + 2Cl - ( ionize to give 3 ions) As the number of ions in solution increases, their conductivity also increases. Therefore, conductivity follows the order: [CO(NH 3 ) 3 Cl 3 ] < [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]Cl < [Co(NH 3 ) 5 Cl]Cl 2 < [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 3
solution_html:
[CO(NH3)3Cl3] - does not ionize
[Co(NH3)4Cl2]Cl = [Co(NH3)4Cl2]+ + Cl- (ionize to give 2 ions)
[Co(NH3)6]Cl3 = [Co(NH3)6]3+ + 3Cl-(ionize to give 4 ions)
[Co(NH3)5Cl]Cl2 = [Co(NH3)5Cl]2+ + 2Cl- ( ionize to give 3 ions)
As the number of ions in solution increases, their conductivity also increases. Therefore, conductivity follows the order:
[CO(NH3)3Cl3] < [Co(NH3)4Cl2]Cl < [Co(NH3)5Cl]Cl2 < [Co(NH3)6]Cl3

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: A coordination compound CrCl 3 4H 2 O precipitates silver chloride when treated with silver nitrate. The molar conductance of its solution corresponds to a total of two ions. Write the structural formula of the compound and name it.
question_html: A coordination compound CrCl34H2O precipitates silver chloride when treated with silver nitrate. The molar conductance of its solution corresponds to a total of two ions. Write the structural formula of the compound and name it.
solution_text: It gives a white precipitate of silver chloride (AgCl) with silver nitrate (AgNO 3 ) it means it has Cl ion outside the coordination sphere. since it gives molar conductance of two ions the formula will be [Co(H 2 O) 4 Cl 2 ]Cl. Its IUPAC name is tetraaquadichloridocobalt (III) chloride.
solution_html: It gives a white precipitate of silver chloride (AgCl) with silver nitrate (AgNO3) it means it has Cl ion outside the coordination sphere. since it gives molar conductance of two ions the formula will be [Co(H2O)4Cl2]Cl. Its IUPAC name is tetraaquadichloridocobalt (III) chloride.

question_text: A complex of the type [M(AA) 2 X 2 ] n+ is known to be optically active. What does this indicate about the structure of the complex? Give one example of such a complex.
question_html: A complex of the type [M(AA)2X2]n+ is known to be optically active. What does this indicate about the structure of the complex? Give one example of such a complex.
solution_text: An optically active complex [M(AA) 2 X 2 ] n+ indicates civ-octahedral structure e.g. cis-[Pt(en) 2 Cl 2 ] 2+ or cis-[Cr(en) 2 Cl 2 ] +
solution_html: An optically active complex  [M(AA)2X2]n+ indicates civ-octahedral structure e.g. cis-[Pt(en)2Cl2]2+ or cis-[Cr(en)2Cl2]+

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Magnetic moment of [MnCl 4 ] 2– is 5.92 BM. Explain giving reason.
question_html: Magnetic moment of [MnCl4]2– is 5.92 BM. Explain giving reason.
solution_text: The spin only magnetic moment [MnCl ​4 ] 2- of 5.92 BM corresponds to the presence of five unpaired electrons in the d-orbitals of Mn 2+ ion. As a result, the hybridization involved in sp rather than dsp 2 . Thus, the tetrahedral structure of [MnCl 4 ] 2- complex will show 5.92 BM spin only magnetic moment value.
solution_html: The spin only magnetic moment [MnCl​4] 2-of 5.92 BM corresponds to the presence of five unpaired electrons in the d-orbitals of Mn2+ ion. As a result, the hybridization involved in sp rather than dsp2. Thus, the tetrahedral structure of [MnCl4]2- complex will show 5.92 BM spin only magnetic moment value.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: On the basis of crystal field theory explain why Co(III) forms a paramagnetic octahedral complex with weak field ligands whereas it forms a diamagnetic octahedral complex with strong field ligands.
question_html: On the basis of crystal field theory explain why Co(III) forms a paramagnetic octahedral complex with weak field ligands whereas it forms a diamagnetic octahedral complex with strong field ligands.
solution_text: With weak ligands {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} < p, the electronic configuration of cobalt Co(lll) will be {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{4} e_{\mathrm{g}}^{2} {/tex} . It has 4 unpaired electrons in 3d shell and is paramagnetic. With strong field ligands {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} > p, the electronic configuration will be {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{6} e_{\mathrm{g}}^{0} {/tex} . It has no unpaired electrons in its shell and is diamagnetic.
solution_html: With weak ligands {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} < p, the electronic configuration of cobalt Co(lll) will be {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{4} e_{\mathrm{g}}^{2} {/tex}. It has 4 unpaired electrons in 3d shell and is paramagnetic. With strong field ligands {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} > p, the electronic configuration will be {tex}t_{2 \mathrm{g}}^{6} e_{\mathrm{g}}^{0} {/tex}. It has no unpaired electrons in its shell and is diamagnetic.

book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes not formed?
question_html: Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes not formed?
solution_text: For tetrahedral complexes, the crystal field stabilization energy is less (not sufficient) and is always lower than pairing energy. {tex}\Delta_{l}=\frac{4}{9} \Delta_{0}{/tex}
solution_html:
For tetrahedral complexes, the crystal field stabilization energy is less (not sufficient) and is always lower than pairing energy.
{tex}\Delta_{l}=\frac{4}{9} \Delta_{0}{/tex}

question_text: Explain why [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ has magnetic moment value of 5.92 BM whereas [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3– has a value of only 1.74 BM.
question_html: Explain why [Fe(H2O)6]3+ has magnetic moment value of 5.92 BM whereas [Fe(CN)6]3– has a value of only 1.74 BM.
solution_text: [Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- : [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ : Due to the presence of 5 unpaired electrons [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 3 has a spin only magnetic moment of 5.92 B.M whereas Fe(CN) 6 ] 3- has 1.74 B.M spin only magnetic moment due to presence of one unpaired electron.
solution_html:
[Fe(CN)6]3-:

[Fe(H2O)6]3+: 

Due to the presence of 5 unpaired electrons [Fe(H2O)6]3 has a spin only magnetic moment of 5.92 B.M whereas Fe(CN)6]3- has 1.74 B.M spin only magnetic moment due to presence of one unpaired electron.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why do compounds having similar geometry have a different magnetic moments?
question_html: Why do compounds having similar geometry have a different magnetic moments?
solution_text: The compounds having similar geometry may have different numbers of unpaired electrons due to the presence of weak and strong field ligands in complexes. If CFSE is high, the complex will show the low value of the magnetic moment and if CFSE is low, the complex will show the high value of the magnetic moment. For example, the [COF 6 ] 3 is paramagnetic with 4 unpaired electrons but [Co(C 2 O 4 ) 3 ] 3+ is diamagnetic.
solution_html: The compounds having similar geometry may have different numbers of unpaired electrons due to the presence of weak and strong field ligands in complexes. If CFSE is high, the complex will show the low value of the magnetic moment and if CFSE is low, the complex will show the high value of the magnetic moment. For example, the [COF6]3 is paramagnetic with 4 unpaired electrons but [Co(C2O4)3]3+ is diamagnetic.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: CuSO 4 .5H 2 O is blue in colour while CuSO 4 is colourless. Why?
question_html: CuSO4.5H2O is blue in colour while CuSO4 is colourless. Why?
solution_text: In CuSO 4 .5H 2 O, water acts as a ligand. As a result, it causes crystal field splitting. Hence, the d-d transition of the electron is possible in CuSO 4 .5H 2 O and shows blue colour. In the anhydrous CuSO 4 due to the absence of water (ligand), crystal field splitting is not possible and hence no d-d transitions of electron and no colour imparted.
solution_html:
In CuSO4.5H2O, water acts as a ligand. As a result, it causes crystal field splitting.
Hence, the d-d transition of the electron is possible in CuSO4.5H2O and shows blue colour. In the anhydrous CuSO4 due to the absence of water (ligand), crystal field splitting is not possible and hence no d-d transitions of electron and no colour imparted.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Name the type of isomerism when ambidentate ligands are attached to the central metal ion. Give two examples of ambidentate ligands.
question_html: Name the type of isomerism when ambidentate ligands are attached to the central metal ion. Give two examples of ambidentate ligands.
solution_text: Linkage isomerism- arises in a coordinated compound containing ambidentate ligand Examples:
solution_html:
Linkage isomerism- arises in a coordinated compound containing ambidentate ligand
Examples:

question_text: Match the complex ions given in Column I with the colours given in Column II and assign the correct code : Column I (Complex ion) Column II (Colour) (a) [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ (i) Violet (b) [Ti(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ (ii) Green (c) [Ni(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ (iii) Pale blue (d) (Ni (H 2 O) 4 (en)] 2+ (aq) (iv) Yellowish orange (v) Blue
question_html:
Match the complex ions given in Column I with the colours given in Column II and assign the correct code :

Column I (Complex ion) Column II (Colour)
(a) [Co(NH3)6]3+ (i) Violet
(b) [Ti(H2O)6]3+ (ii) Green
(c)  [Ni(H2O)6]2+ (iii) Pale blue
(d) (Ni (H2O)4(en)]2+ (aq) (iv) Yellowish orange
  (v) Blue

options.explanation: The complex ion form the corresponding colour are as follow: (a)-(iv), (b)-(iii), (c)-(ii), (d)-(i)

(b) [Co(NH3)4Cl2]SO4 (ii) 0
(c) Na4[Co(S2O3)3] (iii) +1
(d) [Co2(CO)8] (iv) +2
  (v) +3

options.explanation: The oxidation state of cobalt are as follows: [Co(NCS)(NH 3 ) 5 ](SO 3 ) = +2 [Co(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 ]SO 4 = +4 Na 4 [Co(S 2 O 3 ) 3 ] = +3 [Co 2 (CO) 8 ] = +1

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Using crystal field theory, draw energy level diagram, write the electronic configuration of the central metal atom/ion and determine the magnetic moment value in the following: [CoF 6 ] 3- , [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ , [Co(CN) 6 ] 3- [FeF 6 ] 3- , [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ , [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4-
question_html:
Using crystal field theory, draw energy level diagram, write the electronic configuration of the central metal atom/ion and determine the magnetic moment value in the following:

  1. [CoF6]3-, [Co(H2O)6]2+, [Co(CN)6]3-
  2. [FeF6]3-, [Fe(H2O)6]2+, [Fe(CN)6]4-
solution_text: Co 2+ = 3d 7 Number of unpaired electrons = 4 Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex} = 4.9 B.M [Co(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ : Co 2+ = 3d 7 Number of unpaired electrons = 3 Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{3(3+2)}{/tex} = 3.87 B.M [Co(CN) 6 ] 3- : Co 3+ = 3d 6 Number of unpaired electrons = 0 Diamagnetic in nature. {tex}\mathrm{FeF}_{6}^{3-}{/tex} : Fe 2+ = 3d 6 [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ : t 4 2g e g 2 Number of unpaired electrons = 4 Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex} = 4.9 B.M [Fe(CN) 6 ] 4- : Fe 2+ = 3d 6 Diamagnetic in nature.
solution_html:
1. Co2+ = 3d7
    Number of unpaired electrons = 4
    Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex} = 4.9 B.M
    [Co(H2O)6]2+ :

    Co2+ = 3d7
    Number of unpaired electrons = 3
    Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{3(3+2)}{/tex} = 3.87 B.M
    [Co(CN)6]3- :

    Co3+ = 3d6
    Number of unpaired electrons = 0
    Diamagnetic in nature.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{FeF}_{6}^{3-}{/tex} :

    Fe2+ = 3d6
    [Fe(H2O)6]2+ : t42g eg2
    Number of unpaired electrons = 4
    Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex} = 4.9 B.M
    [Fe(CN)6]4- :
     
    Fe2+ = 3d6
    Diamagnetic in nature.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Using valence bond theory, explain the following in relation to the complexes given below: [Mn(CN) 6 ] 3- , [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ , [Cr(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ , [FeCl 6 ] 4- Type of hybridisation. Inner or outer orbital complex. Magnetic behaviour. Spin only magnetic moment value.
question_html:
Using valence bond theory, explain the following in relation to the complexes given below:
[Mn(CN)6]3-, [Co(NH3)6]3+, [Cr(H2O)6]3+ , [FeCl6]4-

  1. Type of hybridisation.
  2. Inner or outer orbital complex.
  3. Magnetic behaviour.
  4. Spin only magnetic moment value.
solution_text: [Mn(CN) 6 ] 3- : Mn 3+ = 3d 4 Hybridisation - d 2 sp 3 Inner orbital complex Paramagnetic in nature. Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{2(2+2)}{/tex} = 2.87 B.M [Co(NH 3 ) 6 ] 3+ : Co 3+ = 3d 6 Hybridisation - d 2 sp 3 Inner orbital complex Paramagnetic in nature Magnetic moment = 0 [Cr(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ : Cr 3+ = 3d 3 Hybridisation - d 2 sp 3 Inner orbital complex Paramagnetic in nature Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{3(3+2)}{/tex} = 3.87 B.M [FeCl 6 ] 4- : Fe 2+ = 3d 6 Hybridisation - sp 3 d 2 Outer orbital complex Paramagnetic in nature Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex} = 4.9 B.M
solution_html:
[Mn(CN)6]3-:
Mn3+ = 3d4 

  1. Hybridisation - d2sp3
  2. Inner orbital complex
  3. Paramagnetic in nature.
  4. Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{2(2+2)}{/tex}  = 2.87 B.M

[Co(NH3)6]3+:
Co3+ = 3d6 

  1. Hybridisation - d2sp3
  2. Inner orbital complex
  3. Paramagnetic in nature
  4. Magnetic moment = 0

[Cr(H2O)6]3+: 
Cr3+ = 3d3 

  1. Hybridisation - d2sp3
  2. Inner orbital complex
  3. Paramagnetic in nature
  4. Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{3(3+2)}{/tex}  = 3.87 B.M

[FeCl6]4-:
Fe2+ = 3d6

  1. Hybridisation - sp3d2
  2. Outer orbital complex
  3. Paramagnetic in nature
  4. Magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex}  = 4.9 B.M

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: CoSO 4 Cl. 5 NH 3 exists in two isomeric forms ‘A’ and ‘B’. Isomer ‘A’ reacts with AgNO 3 to give white precipitate but does not react with BaCl 2 . Isomer ‘B’ gives a white precipitate with BaCl 2 but does not react with AgNO 3 . Answer the following questions. Identify ‘A’ and ‘B’ and write their structural formulas. Name the type of isomerism involved. Give the IUPAC name of ‘A’ and ‘B’.
question_html:
CoSO4Cl.5NH3 exists in two isomeric forms ‘A’ and ‘B’. Isomer ‘A’ reacts with AgNO3 to give white precipitate but does not react with BaCl2. Isomer ‘B’ gives a white precipitate with BaCl2 but does not react with AgNO3. Answer the following questions.

  1. Identify ‘A’ and ‘B’ and write their structural formulas.
  2. Name the type of isomerism involved.
  3. Give the IUPAC name of ‘A’ and ‘B’.
solution_text: CoSO 4 Cl.5NH 3 : Isomer A reacts with AgNO 3 but not with BaCl 2 , it shows that it has Cl ion outside the coordination sphere. Hence, A = [CO(NH 3 ) 5 SO 4 ]CI Isomer B reacts with BaCl 2 but not with AgNO 3 , it shows that it has SO 4 - outside the coordination sphere Hence, B = [CO(NH 3 ) 5 Cl]SO 4 Type of isomerism - Ionization isomerism. IUPAC name of, A = Pentaamminesulphatocobalt (III) chloride and B = Pentaamminesulphatocobalt (III) sulphate.
solution_html:
CoSO4Cl.5NH3:

  1. Isomer A reacts with AgNO3 but not with BaCl2, it shows that it has Cl ion outside the coordination sphere. Hence, A = [CO(NH3)5SO4]CI
    Isomer B reacts with BaCl2 but not with AgNO3, it shows that it has SO4- outside the coordination sphere
    Hence, B = [CO(NH3)5Cl]SO4
  2. Type of isomerism - Ionization isomerism.
  3. IUPAC name of, A = Pentaamminesulphatocobalt (III) chloride and B = Pentaamminesulphatocobalt (III) sulphate.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: What is the relationship between the observed color of the complex and the wavelength of light absorbed by the complex?
question_html: What is the relationship between the observed color of the complex and the wavelength of light absorbed by the complex?
solution_text: When white light falls on the complex, some part of it is absorbed. The higher the crystal field splitting, the lower will be the wavelength absorbed by the complex. The observed color of the complex is the color generated from the wavelength left over.
solution_html: When white light falls on the complex, some part of it is absorbed. The higher the crystal field splitting, the lower will be the wavelength absorbed by the complex. The observed color of the complex is the color generated from the wavelength left over.

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Why are different colors observed in octahedral and tetrahedral complexes for the same metal and same ligands?
question_html: Why are different colors observed in octahedral and tetrahedral complexes for the same metal and same ligands?
solution_text: Lower wavelength is absorbed in the octahedral complex than the tetrahedral complex for the same metal and ligand as the wavelength of light and CFSE are related to each other. In the tetrahedral coordination entity, the formation of the d-orbital splitting is inverted and is smaller as compared to the octahedral field splitting. For the same metal-ligand complexes, it can be shown that {tex}\Delta_{t}=(\frac 4 9) \Delta_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
solution_html: Lower wavelength is absorbed in the octahedral complex than the tetrahedral complex for the same metal and ligand as the wavelength of light and CFSE are related to each other. In the tetrahedral coordination entity, the formation of the d-orbital splitting is inverted and is smaller as compared to the octahedral field splitting. For the same metal-ligand complexes, it can be shown that {tex}\Delta_{t}=(\frac 4 9) \Delta_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
